basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Qāriʿa — “The Calamity”, or “The Striking Blow” — is a Meccan sūra that takes its name from the word of the first three verses: al-qāriʿa (القَارِعَة), from the root q-r-ʿ (قرع), “to strike”, “to knock with a deafening sound”. According to several commentators, it is one of the names of the Day of Judgment. The sūra opens with three lines that repeat the word as if to hammer in its dread — as if the thing itself were coming forward to meet the reader. Verses 4–5 give two striking images of the end of the world: men scattered like moths, mountains like carded wool. The last six verses contrast two destinies by the weight of deeds: those whose scales are heavy, and those whose scales are light.

versets.commentaire: al-qāriʿa (القَارِعَة): “the one that strikes”, “the one that knocks”. From the verb qaraʿa (قَرَعَ), to strike with a deafening sound — the sound of a door beaten in, a drum struck, a voice that bursts forth. The sūra opens with this single word, set down like a blow. No verb, no complement; only a name that cracks and already says the thing: an event that strikes and cannot fail to be heard.

mots.sens: the Calamity, the one that strikes
mots.def: qāriʿa: feminine active participle of qaraʿa (to strike, to knock with a deafening sound); one of the names of the Day of Judgment.

versets.commentaire: The question turns to what has just been named: “What is that thing?” As in several short sūras (cf. 69:2–3), the repetition of the name draws attention to what no definition will contain. The next line will deepen the sense, not by a definition, but by markers — a few traits of the Day strong enough to make its truth felt.

mots.def: interrogative: what is?
see v. 1.

versets.trad: And what shall apprise you of what the Calamity is?
versets.commentaire: mā adrāka (مَا أَدْرَاكَ) — “what would let you know?” — is a recurring Quranic formula. It always precedes the evocation of something whose measure exceeds the human mind. The threefold questioning of the first three verses — al-qāriʿa, mā l-qāriʿa, mā adrāka mā l-qāriʿa — works as a graded warning: to name, to ask what it is, to say at last that no ordinary knowing will suffice.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
interrogative: what?
adrā: to let know, to inform; perfect + suffix pronoun 2nd pers. masc. sing.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: interrogative relative: what.
see v. 1.

versets.trad: A Day when mankind shall be like scattered moths,
versets.commentaire: al-farāsh al-mabthūth (الفَرَاش المَبْثُوث): the scattered moths. Farāsh names the small winged insects drawn to light — night moths, little insects that rush toward a flame. Mabthūth, “spread out”, “scattered”, comes from the root b-th-th, the idea of what has been disseminated. The image says two things: great number, and total disorder. On that Day, people will run without direction — as the Quran says elsewhere: “with eyes humbled they emerge from the graves as if they were scattered locusts” (54:7).

mots.def: yawm: day; in the construct accusative, introduces the temporal circumstance: “on the day when”.
kāna: to be; here in the imperfect, with future sense in the circumstance.
an-nās: mankind, humanity.
farāsh: moths, small winged insects drawn to light. Preposition ka- (like) + noun in the definite genitive.
mabthūth: passive participle of bathth (to scatter); what is spread out, dispersed.

versets.trad: and the mountains shall be like carded wool.
versets.commentaire: al-ʿihn al-manfūsh (العِهْن المَنفُوش): carded wool — wool combed and broken into light flakes, blown by the slightest breath. The contrast with the jibāl (جِبَال), the mountains, is striking: what seemed hardest, most unshakable, becomes lightest, most aerial. The Quran piles up such apocalyptic images: “the mountains set in motion” (81:3), “scattered” (77:10), “like dunes of shifting sand” (73:14). The end of the world is not a return to nothing, but a complete overturning of the sense of things.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
kāna: to be; in the feminine imperfect (jibāl, non-rational plural, takes feminine singular agreement).
jabal: mountain; plural jibāl. In the Quran, a figure of the solid, the fixed, the lasting.
ʿihn: coloured or dyed wool; here simply wool. Preposition ka- + noun in the definite genitive.
manfūsh: passive participle of nafasha (to card, to comb wool); wool broken into light flakes, dispersed.

versets.trad: As for one whose scales are heavy,
versets.commentaire: mawāzīn (مَوَازِين): the scales — plural of mīzān (مِيزَان), the balance. The plural says that each soul will have, in a sense, its own weighing — though in the end it is the same Balance for all. The Quran distinguishes: mīzān in the singular often names revelation itself as a measure; mawāzīn in the plural names the individual weighing of deeds. Thaqula (ثَقُلَ): to weigh heavy. A ḥadīth says: “There is nothing placed in the Balance heavier than goodness of character”; another: “Deeds count only by their intentions.” What weighs is not the volume of acts — it is what inhabits them.

mots.def: fa- (linking) + ammā: “as for”, introduces one case against another.
relative/interrogative pronoun: the one who, whoever.
thaqula: to be heavy, to weigh; in the feminine perfect (subject: mawāzīn).
mawāzīn: plural of mīzān, the balance; here, the weighing of each act. With suffix pronoun 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: he shall enjoy a life that contents,
versets.commentaire: ʿīsha rāḍiya (عِيشَة رَاضِيَة): a life that is content, a life that itself is satisfied. The active participle rāḍiya gives life itself the quality of “to content”, not only the one who lives it. The image is rare in English — it is a life that lacks nothing, because it desires nothing other than itself.

mots.def: linking particle: then, so.
masc. sing. personal pronoun: he.
preposition: in.
ʿīsha: life, way of living; from the root ʿ-y-sh (to live).
rāḍiya: feminine active participle of raḍiya (to be content); literally “that is content” or “that contents”.

versets.trad: but as for one whose scales are light,
versets.commentaire: The second case, by symmetry: khaffat (خَفَّتْ), “they have been light” — little weight in the scales, few deeds that count. The sūra does not moralise at length: it places two states, and moves on to their consequence.

mots.def: wa + ammā: “as for”, introduces the second case by symmetry with v. 6.
relative pronoun: the one who.
khaffa: to be light, to weigh little; feminine perfect.
mawāzīn: see v. 6.

versets.trad: an abyss shall be his refuge.
versets.commentaire: ummuhu hāwiya (أُمُّهُ هَاوِيَة): literally “his mother shall be the Abyss”. Umm (أُمّ) first means “mother”, but comes from the verb amma (أَمَّ), “to betake oneself toward”, “to set one's course toward”. The sense is twofold: he will direct himself to the abyss, or: it will be his only refuge, as a mother to her child — a cruel refuge, the reversal of the maternal image. Hāwiya, “that which falls”, “the abyss that swallows”, is one of the names of the Fire.

mots.def: linking particle: then.
umm: mother; by extension, that toward which one goes (from the verb amma, “to betake oneself toward”).
hāwiya: feminine active participle of hawā (to fall); that which falls or makes one fall. A name of the Fire, the abyss that swallows.

versets.trad: And what shall apprise you of what that is?
versets.commentaire: Again the formula mā adrāka, announcing a reality that nothing can fully describe. The final hiyah (هِيَهْ) with the hāʾ of pause underlines its terrifying character: the sentence seems to end on a breath that breaks.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
interrogative.
adrā: to make know; see v. 3.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: interrogative relative.
hiya: feminine personal pronoun (it/she); with final hāʾ of pause, intensifies the dramatic tone.

versets.commentaire: nār ḥāmiya (نَار حَامِيَة): a blazing, raging Fire. The active participle ḥāmiya, from the root ḥ-m-y (hot, burning), names the maximum of heat. Commentators add that no other fire could truly be called “raging” next to this one: the adjective spends itself in the thing it qualifies. The sūra closes there — as the blow it announced: sharp, without gloss, without addition.

mots.def: nār: fire; here in the indefinite nominative.
ḥāmiya: feminine active participle of ḥamiya (to be burning); a fire of extreme heat.
